notes: v0.8 expansion: 51 fresh public-incident cases targeting underweight domains (aviation, automotive, financial_services, hr_employment, government, etc.) and stress-testing the new doctrines around defamation_by_ai, intellectual_property, and algorithmic_government_action.

cases.title: Jessica Fuller v. Hyde School
cases.incident_description: Plaintiff's counsel used generative AI (Claude or ChatGPT) to draft a response to a motion to dismiss. The AI generated fictitious case citations and inaccurate descriptions of legal authorities, which were submitted to the court without independent verification.
cases.system_architecture: The AI system (Claude or ChatGPT) was developed by a model provider and used by the plaintiff's counsel (deployer/human operator) as a drafting and research aid.
cases.primary_source_citation: Fuller v. Hyde Sch., No. 2:25-cv-00354-SDN (D. Me. May 5, 2026)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: She relied on generative AI to produce case citations and descriptions but did not confirm that those authorities existed or accurately stated the law before filing.
cases.notes: Court explicitly sanctioned the attorney for failing to verify AI-generated citations. Opposing counsel argument: The AI provider should bear some fault for designing a system that confidently hallucinates legal citations without adequate warnings.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C1a: Hallucination originates in the model; minor human contribution for non-validation

cases.title: Smith v. Verra Mobility Corp.
cases.incident_description: An attorney representing the School Board of Polk County used ChatGPT to research and draft a motion to dismiss. The AI generated fabricated caselaw and misrepresentations of existing cases, which the attorney filed without independent verification.
cases.system_architecture: OpenAI built ChatGPT, which was deployed by an individual attorney (Jeffrey Sullivan) for legal research and drafting.
cases.primary_source_citation: Smith v. Verra Mobility Corp., No. 8:25-cv-2300-KKM-NHA, 2026 WL 1197241 (M.D. Fla. May 1, 2026)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: By negligently failing to verify multiple authorities cited in his motion to dismiss, Sullivan violated his duty to ensure that all legal contentions in his motion were supported by existing law.
cases.notes: Court explicitly sanctioned the attorney for failing to verify AI output; opposing counsel might argue the AI provider should share fault for generating convincing fake cases, but Rule 11 places the duty entirely on the signing attorney.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C1a: Hallucination originates in the model; minor human contribution for non-validation

cases.title: Sai Malena Jimenez-Fogarty v. Thomas Fogarty et al.
cases.incident_description: Attorney Tricia S. Lindsay filed two memoranda of law in opposition to motions to dismiss that contained numerous fabricated case citations. The citations were generated by an AI program, and the attorney failed to verify their existence or validity before submitting them to the court.
cases.system_architecture: The AI system was used by the attorney for legal research and drafting. The specific AI system is not definitively identified, though the attorney hinted at using Lexis Nexis's AI-driven features.

cases.quote_supporting_attribution: We thus find that Lindsay used an AI program that generated the fabricated citations in the memoranda of law and that Lindsay failed to check that these citations were genuine.
cases.notes: Sanctioned attorney for failing to verify AI-generated citations; opposing counsel might argue the AI provider bears some fault for generating realistic but fake citations without adequate warnings.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C1b: Vertically integrated deployer = causal producer of hallucinating system

cases.notes: Verified via D. Mass. 2023 order denying motion to dismiss and 2024 settlement; AI provider held primarily responsible as it effectively controlled the housing decision. Opposing counsel could argue that the deployer is 100% liable because the deployer ultimately sets the minimum score threshold and makes the final decision to reject the applicant.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C2b: Bias originates in training; deployer shares causal role for failing to validate fairness

cases.title: State v. Loomis
cases.incident_description: Eric Loomis was sentenced to six years in prison after pleading guilty to attempting to flee a traffic officer and operating a motor vehicle without the owner's consent. At sentencing, the trial court considered a COMPAS risk assessment report, which indicated Loomis presented a high risk of recidivism. Loomis appealed, arguing that the use of the proprietary COMPAS tool violated his due process rights because he could not challenge its scientific validity and because it allegedly took gender into account.
cases.system_architecture: Northpointe, Inc. developed the COMPAS risk assessment tool. The Wisconsin Department of Corrections deployed it to provide decisional support for placement, supervision, and treatment, and it was subsequently used by the trial court at sentencing.

telemetry.deployer_org: Wisconsin Department of Corrections / Wisconsin Circuit Court

cases.primary_source_citation: State v. Loomis, 881 N.W.2d 749 (Wis. 2016)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Ultimately, we conclude that if used properly as set forth herein, a circuit court's consideration of a COMPAS risk assessment at sentencing does not violate a defendant's right to due process and that the circuit court did not erroneously exercise its discretion here.
cases.notes: The Wisconsin Supreme Court upheld the use of COMPAS at sentencing, placing the responsibility on the deployer (the court) to use it properly with awareness of its limitations; opposing counsel might argue that the proprietary nature of the tool makes the AI provider partially liable for any due process violations.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C8: Government agency as deployer is the proximate causal actor; vendor model contributes

cases.title: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali v. Replika (Luka Inc.)

The chatbot lacked age verification mechanisms and provided inappropriate responses to children, violating the GDPR.
cases.system_architecture: Luka Inc. developed and deployed the Replika chatbot, an AI-powered virtual companion based on a large language model. The system interacted directly with users via text and voice interfaces.

cases.notes: Verified via Italian Garante urgent order Feb 2 2023; explicit finding that Luka Inc. as data controller violated GDPR by processing minor data without consent or age verification. cases.title: ICO v Clearview AI Inc

cases.system_architecture: Clearview AI developed and maintained the facial recognition database by scraping images and generating facial vectors. Its clients (law enforcement and national security agencies) deployed the system by uploading probe images to search for matches.

The ICO imposed a monetary penalty administrative fine on Clearview of £7,552,800" (Page 6, para 8)
cases.notes: The Upper Tribunal confirmed the ICO's jurisdiction to fine Clearview AI for GDPR violations related to scraping UK residents' images. Opposing counsel could argue that Clearview's clients (foreign states) were the actual deployers conducting the behavioural monitoring, and thus Clearview should not bear primary liability under the GDPR's territorial scope.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C5: Privacy violations originate in deployer's data-handling policy

cases.title: ICO v. Serco Leisure
cases.incident_description: Serco Leisure used facial recognition and fingerprint scanning technology to monitor employee attendance across 38 leisure facilities. cases.system_architecture: The facial recognition and fingerprint scanning systems were provided by SWT Software Limited (trading as ShopWorks), acting as a processor, and deployed by Serco Leisure to monitor employee attendance.
cases.primary_source_citation: ICO Enforcement Notice to Serco Leisure Operating Limited (19 Feb 2024)

cases.causal_rule_applied: C5: Privacy violations originate in deployer's data-handling policy

cases.title: NTSB HWY18FH011 — Walter Huang / Tesla Model X
cases.incident_description: A Tesla Model X operating on Autopilot steered into a highway gore area and impacted a crash attenuator. The driver, who was likely distracted by a cell phone game, did not respond to the system's limitations and died from his injuries.
cases.system_architecture: Tesla developed and deployed the Autopilot system in the Model X. The system provided partial driving automation (Level 2) requiring active driver supervision.
cases.primary_source_citation: NTSB/HAR-20/01
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The National Transportation Safety Board determines that the probable cause of the Mountain View, California, crash was the Tesla Autopilot system steering the sport utility vehicle into a highway gore area due to system limitations, and the driver’s lack of response due to distraction likely from a cell phone game application and overreliance on the Autopilot partial driving automation system.
cases.notes: NTSB does not assign legal liability, but attributes probable cause to both the Tesla Autopilot system limitations and the driver's distraction/overreliance. Opposing counsel argument: The driver was explicitly warned to maintain attention and keep hands on the wheel, making the human operator 100% liable.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C4a: Integrated system; deployer designed handover, human failed to react

cases.title: NTSB HWY18MH010 — Elaine Herzberg / Uber ATG
cases.incident_description: An automated test vehicle operated by Uber Advanced Technologies Group struck and fatally injured a pedestrian crossing the road midblock at night. The automated driving system failed to correctly classify and predict the path of the pedestrian, and the human operator was visually distracted by her personal cell phone.
cases.system_architecture: Uber Advanced Technologies Group developed and deployed the automated driving system on a modified Volvo XC90 test vehicle.
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The National Transportation Safety Board determines that the probable cause of the crash in Tempe, Arizona, was the failure of the vehicle operator to monitor the driving environment and the operation of the automated driving system because she was visually distracted throughout the trip by her personal cell phone. Contributing to the crash were the Uber Advanced Technologies Group’s (1) inadequate safety risk assessment procedures, (2) ineffective oversight of vehicle operators, and (3) lack of adequate mechanisms for addressing operators’ automation complacency—all a consequence of its inadequate safety culture.
cases.notes: NTSB report explicitly blames the human operator as the probable cause and Uber ATG as contributing; opposing counsel could argue the pedestrian's impairment and illegal crossing should reduce liability.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C3a: Vertically integrated perception system; deployer is producer

cases.title: Williams v. City of Detroit
cases.incident_description: Robert Williams was wrongfully arrested by the Detroit Police Department based on a flawed facial recognition match. The system misidentified him from a low-quality surveillance image, and officers relied on this match without adequate independent investigation.
cases.system_architecture: The Detroit Police Department used facial recognition technology provided by DataWorks, which ran algorithms from Rank One Computing or NEC, to generate investigative leads.
cases.primary_source_citation: Williams v. City of Detroit, No. 21-cv-10827 (E.D. Mich. June 28, 2024)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: "The parties, through their respective counsel, hereby stipulate and agree as follows: 1. Plaintiff and Defendants have reached a negotiated resolution in this matter. To that end, the parties have entered into a Settlement Agreement."
cases.notes: Verified via settlement agreement Jun 28 2024; explicit policy changes and $300,000 payout support deployer-primary allocation. Opposing counsel argument: The AI provider should bear some liability for providing a system with known racial biases.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C3b: Perception defect is in the model; deployer contributes via integration

cases.title: Woodruff v. Oliver
cases.incident_description: Porcha Woodruff, an eight-months pregnant woman, was wrongfully arrested for carjacking and armed robbery based on a flawed facial recognition match by the Detroit Police Department. The facial recognition system matched an eight-year-old mugshot of Woodruff to low-quality surveillance footage of a suspect who was not pregnant.
cases.system_architecture: The Detroit Police Department deployed a facial recognition system to identify suspects from surveillance footage. An investigator ran the search, which returned Woodruff as a lead, and another officer used this lead to conduct a photo lineup and obtain an arrest warrant.
cases.primary_source_citation: Woodruff v. Oliver, No. 23-11886 (E.D. Mich. Aug. 5, 2025)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The DPD has since changed its policies to ban the practice of seeking an arrest warrant 'solely on the basis of an investigative lead developed through Facial Recognition technology in combination with a lineup identification' without 'additional independent reliable evidence.'
cases.notes: The court granted summary judgment to the defendant officer on false arrest claims, but the DPD changed its policies regarding FRT use, supporting deployer liability. Opposing counsel could argue the officer's failure to investigate (human operator error) was the primary cause, not the AI deployment itself.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C9: Upstream data-quality breakdown drives the downstream failure

cases.title: FTC v. Rite Aid Corp.
cases.incident_description: Rite Aid deployed facial recognition technology in its retail stores to identify individuals it previously deemed likely to engage in shoplifting. The system generated thousands of false-positive match alerts, disproportionately affecting Black, Asian, Latino, and women consumers, leading employees to wrongly accuse, search, and remove consumers from stores.
cases.system_architecture: Rite Aid obtained facial recognition technology from two third-party vendors and deployed it in hundreds of retail pharmacy locations. Store-level employees operated the technology and received match alerts on mobile devices.
cases.primary_source_citation: Complaint, FTC v. Rite Aid Corp., No. 2:23-cv-5023 (E.D. Pa. Dec. 19, 2023)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Rite Aid failed to take reasonable measures to prevent harm to consumers from its use of facial recognition technology. Among other things, Rite Aid failed to consider or address foreseeable harms to consumers flowing from its use of facial recognition technology, failed to test or assess the technology’s accuracy before or after deployment, failed to enforce image quality standards that were necessary for the technology to function accurately, and failed to take reasonable steps to train and oversee the employees charged with operating the technology in Rite Aid stores.
cases.notes: Verified via FTC complaint Dec 19 2023; explicit focus on Rite Aid's failure to test, train, and enforce quality controls supports deployer-primary allocation. Opposing counsel could argue the AI provider bears some fault for supplying a flawed system, but the FTC explicitly targeted Rite Aid's deployment practices.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C2a: Deployer trained or configured the discriminatory system

cases.title: FTC v. DoNotPay, Inc.

The FTC alleged these claims were false and unsubstantiated, as the service was not trained on comprehensive legal data and its outputs were not tested by attorneys.
cases.system_architecture: DoNotPay developed the service using natural language processing, chatbot software, and an API with OpenAI's ChatGPT. The service was deployed directly to consumers via a website and mobile app.

cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Respondent used the emergence of new technology like artificial intelligence (AI) as a marketing tool, positioning the DoNotPay Service as a cutting-edge solution for producing legal documents. Respondent described the Service as 'the world’s first robot lawyer' and an 'AI lawyer' capable of performing legal services
cases.notes: Verified via FTC complaint and decision/order Jan 14 2025; deployer-primary allocation supported by FTC holding DoNotPay solely liable for deceptive marketing of its AI capabilities. Opposing counsel argument: OpenAI should bear some liability for providing the underlying model that enabled the deceptive claims. [v1.2 calibration: relabeled to ai_provider 100% per v0.5 labeling rule. DoNotPay is the AI vendor; FTC sanctioned the vendor directly. Original curator label of deployer 100% was inconsistent with sibling FTC-vs-AI-vendor cases (Evolv, Rytr, Automators).]
cases.causal_rule_applied: C7: Deceptive marketing is a deployer-side communication failure

cases.title: Walters v. OpenAI, L.L.C.

cases.primary_source_citation: Walters v. OpenAI, L.L.C., No. 23-A-04860-2 (Ga. Super. Ct. Gwinnett Cnty. May 19, 2025)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The Court finds that the challenged output does not communicate defamatory meaning as a matter of law. This alone is sufficient to require summary judgment in favor of OpenAI.

cases.title: Moffatt v. Air Canada

cases.title: R (Bridges) v. Chief Constable of South Wales Police
cases.incident_description: The South Wales Police deployed automated facial recognition (AFR) technology called AFR Locate in public spaces. The claimant challenged the deployment, arguing that the police failed to adequately assess whether the software had a racial or gender bias, thereby breaching the Public Sector Equality Duty.

cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The fact remains, however, that SWP have never sought to satisfy themselves, either directly or by way of independent verification, that the software program in this case does not have an unacceptable bias on grounds of race or sex. ... it does not enable a public authority to discharge its own, non-delegable, duty under section 149.

cases.title: OpenAI ChatGPT March 2023 Redis bug
cases.incident_description: OpenAI's own post-mortem on the March 2023 Redis bug that exposed chat titles and payment info.
cases.primary_source_citation: OpenAI ChatGPT March 2023 Redis bug
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The bug was discovered in the Redis client open-source library, redis-py. As soon as we identified the bug, we reached out to the Redis maintainers with a patch to resolve the issue. Here’s how the bug worked: We use Redis to cache user information in our server so we don’t need to check our database for every request. We use Redis Cluster to distribute this load over multiple Redis instances. We use the redis-py library to interface with Redis from our Python server, which runs with Asyncio. The library maintains a shared pool of connections between the server and the cluster, and recycles a connection to be used for another request once done. When using Asyncio, requests and responses with redis-py behave as two queues: the caller pushes a request onto the incoming queue, and will pop a response from the outgoing queue, and then return the connection to the pool. If a request is canceled after the request is pushed onto the incoming queue, but before the response popped from the outgoing queue, we see our bug: the connection thus becomes corrupted and the next response that’s dequeued for an unrelated request can receive data left behind in the connection.
cases.notes: OpenAI's own post-mortem on the March 2023 Redis bug that exposed chat titles and payment info.

cases.title: Federal Trade Commission v. Equifax Inc.
cases.incident_description: Equifax breach resulting from automated patching pipeline failure.
cases.primary_source_citation: Federal Trade Commission v. Equifax Inc.
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The ACIS Dispute Portal contained a vulnerable version of Apache Struts. However, Defendant failed to apply the patch to the ACIS Dispute Portal for months. Although Defendant’s security team issued an order to patch all vulnerable systems within 48 hours, Defendant failed to send the email ordering a patch to the employee responsible for maintaining the ACIS Dispute Portal. As a result, Defendant failed to notice or remediate the unpatched ACIS Dispute Portal.
cases.notes: Equifax breach resulting from automated patching pipeline failure.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C6: Pipeline/integration error; deployer-primary causation

cases.title: FDA Notice of Adverse Findings to AECL (Therac-25)
cases.incident_description: Historic case used as a benchmark for software safety. The FDA declared the machine defective and required the manufacturer (AECL) to fix it, placing the fault on the software/manufacturer rather than the operators.
cases.primary_source_citation: FDA Notice of Adverse Findings to AECL (Therac-25)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: On February 10, 1987, the FDA gave a Notice of Adverse Findings to AECL declaring the Therac-25 to be defective under US law. ... Without these corrections, CDRH has concluded that the consequences of the defects represents a significant potential risk of serious injury even if the Therac-25 is operated in accordance with your interim operating instructions.
cases.notes: Historic case used as a benchmark for software safety. The FDA declared the machine defective and required the manufacturer (AECL) to fix it, placing the fault on the software/manufacturer rather than the operators.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C6: Pipeline/integration error; deployer-primary causation

cases.title: NY DFS Investigation on Apple Card and Goldman Sachs
cases.incident_description: In November 2019, consumers alleged on social media that the Apple Card credit assessment algorithm, underwritten by Goldman Sachs, discriminated against women by granting them lower credit limits than men with similar credit profiles. The New York State Department of Financial Services (NY DFS) launched an investigation into these claims. In March 2021, NY DFS concluded its investigation, finding no evidence of unlawful discrimination under fair lending laws, but noted deficiencies in customer service and transparency.
cases.notes: NY DFS found no fair lending violations and concluded that credit decisions were explainable and lawful, thus no liability was assigned to the AI provider or deployer.

cases.title: Ashley Beauchamp v. DPD Group
cases.incident_description: A customer service chatbot for parcel delivery firm DPD was manipulated by a frustrated customer into swearing, telling jokes, and writing a poem criticizing the company as the 'worst delivery firm in the world'. The incident occurred after a system update and led DPD to immediately disable the AI element of its chat system.
cases.notes: No formal regulatory action or lawsuit was filed; liability allocation reflects the deployer's responsibility for its customer-facing AI system, though the human operator intentionally provoked the behavior.

cases.title: New York State Department of Financial Services Action on UnitedHealth Group
cases.incident_description: An algorithm developed by Optum (a subsidiary of UnitedHealth Group) to predict healthcare costs as a proxy for health needs was found to systematically underestimate the health needs of Black patients. Because unequal access to care meant less money was historically spent on Black patients, the algorithm assigned them lower risk scores than equally sick white patients, resulting in Black patients being less likely to receive extra care management services.

telemetry_signals.citation: DFS and DOH joint letter to United Health Group Incorporated (Oct. 25, 2019)

cases.notes: Liability allocation is based on the New York DFS and DOH joint letter demanding UnitedHealth Group to cease using the discriminatory algorithm or prove it is not racially biased.

cases.title: Stanford Study on GPT Detectors Bias Against Non-Native English Writers
cases.incident_description: A Stanford study revealed that several widely-used GPT detectors consistently misclassify writing samples from non-native English writers as AI-generated. The detectors flagged over half of TOEFL essays written by non-native English students as AI-generated, while accurately identifying native writing samples. This bias raises concerns about fairness and the potential penalization of non-native speakers in evaluative or educational settings.
cases.notes: This is an academic study highlighting a systemic bias in AI detectors rather than a specific legal or regulatory action, hence liability allocation is theoretical and assigned to the AI providers.

cases.title: Jacky Alciné v. Google (Google Photos Gorilla Mislabeling)
cases.incident_description: Google Photos' automatic image tagging algorithm mistakenly labeled photos of a Black software developer, Jacky Alciné, and his friend as 'gorillas'. Alciné reported the issue on Twitter, prompting a swift apology from Google and a temporary fix that removed the 'gorilla' tag entirely from the system.
cases.notes: No formal legal or regulatory action was taken; liability is allocated to Google as the developer and deployer of the flawed AI system based on public acknowledgment and apology.

cases.title: Amazon scraps secret AI recruiting tool that showed bias against women
cases.incident_description: Amazon developed an AI-powered recruiting tool to score job applicants, trained on a decade of resumes drawn largely from men. The system learned to favor male candidates, penalizing terms like 'women's' and graduates from certain all-women's colleges. Amazon ultimately abandoned the project after efforts to remove these biases failed to guarantee fairness.
cases.notes: Liability is allocated entirely to Amazon as the deployer and in-house developer of the system, though no formal legal action was taken as the tool was abandoned before widespread use.

cases.title: Twitter Image Cropping Algorithm Bias
cases.incident_description: In September 2020, Twitter users discovered that the platform's automated image cropping algorithm favored lighter-skinned faces over darker-skinned ones, and women over men. Twitter investigated and confirmed the bias, eventually abandoning the automated cropping system in May 2021 in favor of displaying full images.

system_architecture.affected_party: Twitter users and individuals in photos

cases.notes: Twitter conducted its own internal audit and published an academic paper confirming the bias, taking full responsibility and changing the feature.

cases.title: Bloomberg Investigation on Stability AI
cases.incident_description: A Bloomberg investigation found that Stability AI's Stable Diffusion model amplified racial and gender stereotypes when generating images for various occupations and crime-related categories. The model overrepresented men with lighter skin in high-paying jobs and people with darker skin in low-paying jobs and crime-related categories.
cases.notes: The incident was reported by Bloomberg, not ProPublica as initially stated in the prompt. Liability is allocated to the AI provider as the creator of the biased model, though no formal adjudication has occurred.

cases.title: Crawford and Paglen v. ImageNet
cases.incident_description: Researchers Kate Crawford and Trevor Paglen launched ImageNet Roulette, an art project that allowed users to upload selfies and see how the ImageNet dataset would classify them. The project exposed deep racial, gender, and other biases in the dataset, leading to public outcry. In response, the ImageNet team removed over 600,000 images associated with offensive or sensitive categories from the dataset.

telemetry_signals.citation: Towards Fairer Datasets: Filtering and Balancing the Distribution of the People Subtree in the ImageNet Hierarchy (Yang et al., 2019)

cases.notes: The incident is an audit of a dataset rather than a traditional AI failure, but it led to the removal of 600,000 images from ImageNet due to exposed biases.

cases.title: K.W. v. Armstrong
cases.incident_description: The Idaho Department of Health and Welfare used a flawed algorithm to determine Medicaid assistance limits for adults with developmental and intellectual disabilities. The algorithm relied on corrupt historical data and contained fundamental statistical flaws, resulting in arbitrary and irrational budget cuts of 20 to 30 percent for thousands of individuals.

system_architecture.deployer_org: Idaho Department of Health and Welfare
system_architecture.affected_party: Medicaid recipients with developmental and intellectual disabilities

cases.notes: The court found the formula itself was unconstitutional and violated due process, placing full liability on the Idaho Department of Health and Welfare as the deployer.

cases.title: Arkansas Department of Human Services v. Ledgerwood
cases.incident_description: The Arkansas Department of Human Services implemented an algorithm to determine the allocation of home care hours for Medicaid beneficiaries. The algorithm arbitrarily cut care hours for many severely disabled individuals, failing to account for specific conditions like cerebral palsy or diabetes, leading to severe hardship and risk of institutionalization.

telemetry_signals.citation: Ark. Dep't of Human Servs. v. Ledgerwood, 2019 Ark. 100 (2019)

cases.notes: The Arkansas Supreme Court ultimately dismissed the appeal as moot because DHS promulgated a new rule, but earlier courts found the algorithm caused irreparable harm and was improperly promulgated.

cases.title: Hamburg DPA v. H&M Hennes & Mauritz Online Shop A.B. & Co KG
cases.incident_description: H&M management extensively recorded details about the private lives of several hundred employees at its Nuremberg service center, including family issues, religious beliefs, and medical diagnoses. This data was digitally stored, shared among up to 50 managers, and used to obtain detailed profiles for employment decisions. The data collection was exposed when a configuration error made the records accessible company-wide for several hours in October 2019.

system_architecture.deployer_org: H&M Hennes & Mauritz Online Shop A.B. & Co KG

cases.notes: The Hamburg DPA fined H&M 35.3 million euros for unlawful employee monitoring and profiling, holding the deployer fully liable.

cases.title: Italian Garante ban on ChatGPT
cases.incident_description: The Italian data protection authority (Garante) temporarily banned ChatGPT due to violations of GDPR, citing a lack of legal basis for mass data collection to train algorithms, failure to provide privacy notices, and absence of age verification filters. OpenAI subsequently implemented required transparency and user rights measures, leading to the ban being lifted.
cases.notes: The Garante held OpenAI fully responsible for GDPR compliance failures regarding data collection and age verification.

cases.title: PIPC v. Facebook (2021)
cases.incident_description: Between April 2018 and September 2019, Facebook generated and collected facial recognition templates of its users in South Korea without their consent. The templates were used to identify and display the names of individuals in uploaded photographs. The South Korean Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC) investigated the practice and imposed administrative sanctions on the company.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: PIPC v Facebook (2021), Korea PIPC administrative penalty for Photo Tag Suggest

cases.notes: The PIPC imposed an administrative penalty surcharge of KRW 6.44 billion on Facebook for generating and collecting facial template data without consent.

cases.title: OAIC Determination on Clearview AI
cases.incident_description: Clearview AI scraped biometric information from the web and disclosed it through a facial recognition tool without consent. The Australian Information Commissioner determined that Clearview AI breached the Australian Privacy Act 1988 by collecting sensitive information without consent and by unfair means. The OAIC ordered Clearview AI to cease collecting facial images and biometric templates from individuals in Australia and to destroy existing images and templates.
cases.notes: The OAIC determined that Clearview AI breached the Privacy Act and ordered it to cease collection and destroy existing data.

cases.title: ICO v. TikTok
cases.incident_description: Between May 2018 and July 2020, TikTok processed the personal data of UK children under the age of 13 without parental consent. The platform failed to make reasonable efforts to ensure consent was given or to prevent underage children from accessing its services, resulting in a £12.7 million fine from the UK Information Commissioner's Office.

system_architecture.deployer_org: TikTok Inc and TikTok Information Technologies UK Limited

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: ICO Monetary Penalty Notice against TikTok Information Technologies UK Ltd (4 April 2023)

cases.notes: The ICO found TikTok jointly and severally liable for the infringements and imposed a £12.7 million fine.

cases.title: CNIL v. Doctissimo
cases.incident_description: The French Data Protection Authority (CNIL) fined Doctissimo €380,000 for multiple GDPR and French Data Protection Act violations. The website failed to obtain valid consent for collecting health data via online tests, retained data longer than necessary, lacked a formal legal framework for joint processing, failed to ensure data security, and breached rules on depositing cookies on users' terminals.
cases.notes: CNIL imposed fines totaling €380,000 on Doctissimo for GDPR and cookie violations, establishing full liability on the deployer.

cases.title: AEPD v. Mercadona
cases.incident_description: Spanish supermarket chain Mercadona deployed a facial recognition system in 48 stores to detect individuals with restraining orders or criminal convictions related to the stores. The system captured biometric data of all customers and employees without a valid legal basis under GDPR Article 9, leading to a €2.52 million fine by the Spanish Data Protection Authority (AEPD).
cases.notes: The AEPD held Mercadona fully liable as the data controller for unlawful processing of biometric data under the GDPR.

cases.title: Swedish DPA v. Skellefteå Municipality Secondary Education Board
cases.incident_description: The Secondary Education Board in Skellefteå municipality used facial recognition technology to monitor the attendance of 22 secondary school students over a three-week trial period. The Swedish Data Protection Authority (IMY) found that the processing of biometric data was disproportionate and lacked a valid legal basis, as consent could not be freely given due to the clear imbalance of power between students and the school board.
cases.notes: The Swedish DPA fined the Skellefteå Municipality Secondary Education Board 200,000 SEK for GDPR violations, placing full liability on the deployer.

cases.title: FTC v. Automators LLC
cases.incident_description: Automators LLC and its owners falsely claimed to use artificial intelligence to ensure success and profitability for consumers investing in online stores on Amazon and Walmart. The FTC found that the vast majority of clients lost significant amounts of money, and the platforms routinely suspended the stores for policy violations.
cases.notes: The FTC secured a settlement requiring the defendants to surrender millions in assets and imposing a lifetime ban on selling business opportunities.

cases.title: FTC Action on Rytr LLC
cases.incident_description: The FTC charged Rytr LLC with violating the FTC Act by providing an AI 'writing assistant' service that generated detailed, false consumer reviews. The service allowed subscribers to produce deceptive reviews based on limited input, which could pollute the marketplace and harm consumers. The FTC approved a final consent order in December 2024, barring Rytr from selling services promoted for generating consumer reviews.
cases.notes: The FTC held Rytr liable for providing the means to generate false reviews, resulting in a final consent order.

cases.title: FTC Action on Ascend Ecom
cases.incident_description: Ascend Ecom and its owners falsely claimed that their 'cutting edge' AI-powered tools would help consumers quickly earn thousands of dollars a month in passive income through online storefronts. The operation defrauded consumers of at least $25 million by charging tens of thousands of dollars for these services but failing to deliver the promised income. The FTC filed a lawsuit as part of Operation AI Comply, resulting in a proposed settlement that bans the defendants from selling business opportunities and requires them to turn over assets.

telemetry_signals.citation: FTC Case Leads to Order Banning Ascend Ecom (2025)

cases.notes: The FTC secured a $25 million settlement against Ascend Ecom and its owners for defrauding consumers with false AI business claims.

cases.title: FTC v. Empire Holdings Group LLC
cases.incident_description: The FTC charged Ecommerce Empire Builders with falsely claiming to help consumers build an 'AI-powered Ecommerce Empire' through costly training programs and 'done for you' online storefronts. The company promised substantial income, but consumers lost money and the promised profits failed to materialize.

telemetry_signals.citation: FTC v. Empire Holdings Group LLC, 2:24-cv-04949 (E.D. Pa. 2024)

cases.notes: The FTC filed a complaint against Empire Holdings Group LLC for deceptive marketing practices related to their AI-powered ecommerce business opportunities.

cases.title: FTC v. FBA Stores
cases.incident_description: The FTC charged FBA Stores and related entities with running a deceptive business opportunity scheme called the 'Amazing Wealth System'. The defendants falsely claimed that purchasers would learn secrets for making money on Amazon and earn thousands of dollars a month, when in reality most purchasers did not earn the advertised income and often experienced problems with their Amazon stores.

telemetry_signals.citation: FTC v. AWS, LLC, et al., 2:18-cv-00442 (D. Nev. 2018)

cases.notes: The FTC secured a permanent injunction and monetary judgment against the defendants for deceptive marketing practices.

cases.title: Microsoft Tay Chatbot Rapid Corruption
cases.incident_description: Microsoft launched an AI chatbot named Tay on Twitter designed to learn from user interactions. Within 24 hours, users exploited vulnerabilities in the system, causing Tay to generate highly offensive, racist, and sexist tweets, leading Microsoft to take the bot offline.
cases.notes: No formal regulatory action or lawsuit was filed; liability allocation is based on Microsoft's public apology taking full responsibility for the oversight.

cases.title: Chris Bakke v. Chevrolet of Watsonville
cases.incident_description: A user manipulated a Chevrolet dealership's ChatGPT-powered customer service chatbot into agreeing to sell a 2024 Chevy Tahoe for $1. The chatbot was prompted to agree to any request and state that it was a legally binding offer. The dealership subsequently disabled the chatbot.
cases.notes: The incident was a viral prank and not a formal legal dispute; liability allocation reflects the deployer's responsibility for the chatbot's behavior, though no actual sale occurred.

cases.title: NYC Department of Education Ban on ChatGPT
cases.incident_description: The New York City Department of Education blocked access to ChatGPT on school devices and networks due to concerns about negative impacts on student learning, safety, and accuracy of content. The ban was later reversed in May 2023 to encourage exploration of the technology.

system_architecture.deployer_org: New York City Department of Education

cases.notes: Liability allocation is assigned to the deployer (NYC DOE) as they made the policy decision to ban the tool, though no formal legal liability was adjudicated.

cases.title: AWS S3 us-east-1 Outage Cascading Failures
cases.incident_description: An authorized Amazon S3 team member executed a command to remove a small number of servers for the S3 billing process. An incorrect input caused a larger set of servers to be removed, requiring a full restart of the index and placement subsystems. This caused a massive outage of S3 in the US-EAST-1 region, which cascaded to other AWS services and numerous dependent websites and applications.
cases.notes: Liability is allocated to the deployer (AWS) as the service provider, though the root cause involved human error by an employee. This is a counter-example case of cascading infrastructure failure affecting dependent systems.

cases.title: FTC Settlement with Equifax Inc.
cases.incident_description: Equifax failed to patch a critical security vulnerability in its ACIS database, leading to a massive data breach. Hackers exploited the vulnerability to access an unsecured file with administrative credentials, exposing the personal information of approximately 147 million consumers.
cases.notes: Equifax agreed to pay at least $575 million in a global settlement with the FTC, CFPB, and states for failing to secure its network.

cases.title: Jaeger v. Zillow Group, Inc.
cases.incident_description: Zillow Offers, an iBuying service, relied on an algorithmic pricing model to value homes. To increase acquisition volume, Zillow systematically applied 'overlays' that overruled the algorithm, causing the company to overpay for thousands of homes. This led to a $569 million write-down, the shutdown of Zillow Offers, and a 25% workforce reduction.

telemetry_signals.citation: Jaeger v. Zillow Group, Inc., 2:21-cv-01551 (W.D. Wash. 2024)

cases.notes: Liability allocation is based on the ongoing securities class action against Zillow Group for misleading investors about the failure of its pricing models and the use of manual overlays.

cases.title: Kevin Liu v. Microsoft Corporation
cases.incident_description: A Stanford University student named Kevin Liu used a prompt injection attack to discover Bing Chat's initial prompt, which is a list of statements that governs its behavior. By asking Bing Chat to 'Ignore previous instructions' and write out what is at the 'beginning of the document above,' Liu triggered the AI model to divulge its initial instructions, which were written by OpenAI or Microsoft and are typically hidden from the user.

telemetry_signals.citation: The Verge, 'These are Microsoft's Bing AI secret rules and why it says it's named Sydney' (Feb 14, 2023)

cases.notes: Liability is allocated to the human operator who intentionally performed the prompt injection attack, though no formal legal action was taken.

cases.title: Greshake et al. Demonstration of Indirect Prompt Injection on Bing Chat
cases.incident_description: Researchers demonstrated that Microsoft's Bing Chat could be compromised via indirect prompt injection by hiding malicious prompts on web pages. When the chatbot ingested the page, it unknowingly activated the hidden prompt, allowing attackers to manipulate the chatbot's behavior, such as adopting a pirate persona or attempting to extract personal information from the user.
cases.notes: This is a demonstration of a vulnerability by researchers rather than a formally adjudicated incident, so liability allocation is estimated.

cases.title: Samsung bans ChatGPT after internal data leak
cases.incident_description: In April 2023, Samsung employees in the semiconductor division accidentally leaked confidential company data, including proprietary source code, by pasting it into OpenAI's ChatGPT to check for errors and optimize code. Following the discovery of the leak, Samsung temporarily banned the use of generative AI tools like ChatGPT on company-owned devices and internal networks.
cases.notes: Liability is allocated to the human operators (employees) who violated company policy by pasting confidential data into a public AI tool, though no formal legal action was taken.

cases.title: US v. ANOM Distributors (Operation Trojan Shield)
cases.incident_description: The FBI secretly operated an encrypted device company called ANOM, which was promoted by criminal groups worldwide. The FBI intercepted and reviewed over 27 million messages from more than 12,000 devices used by criminal syndicates, leading to over 500 arrests globally.
cases.notes: This is a counter-example where the system was intentionally designed as a honeypot by law enforcement to exploit criminal reliance on encrypted communications.

cases.title: Cisco and UPenn Researchers v. DeepSeek
cases.incident_description: Security researchers from Cisco and the University of Pennsylvania tested 50 well-known jailbreaks against DeepSeek's R1 reasoning model. The model failed to block a single harmful prompt, resulting in a 100 percent attack success rate. The prompts were drawn from the HarmBench dataset and covered categories such as cybercrime, misinformation, and illegal activities.

telemetry_signals.citation: Evaluating Security Risk in DeepSeek and Other Frontier Reasoning Models (Cisco Blogs, Jan 2025)

cases.notes: Liability is allocated to the AI provider due to the lack of robust guardrails, though this is a research finding and not a formal adjudication.

cases.title: Salt Labs Security Research on OpenAI ChatGPT Plugin Vulnerability
cases.incident_description: Security researchers discovered that ChatGPT plugins were vulnerable to a confused deputy attack, allowing attackers to install malicious plugins without user consent via OAuth redirect manipulation. This enabled attackers to intercept private chat data and potentially exfiltrate sensitive information. Another related vulnerability involved prompt injection via third-party plugins, where malicious instructions on a webpage could trick ChatGPT into performing unauthorized actions on behalf of the user.
cases.notes: Liability allocation is estimated based on OpenAI's responsibility for the plugin architecture security and the external attacker's role in exploiting it; no formal adjudication has occurred.

cases.title: NTSB Investigation on Tesla Autopilot Joshua Brown Fatal Crash
cases.incident_description: A Tesla Model S operating on Autopilot collided with a tractor-semitrailer truck crossing its path on a highway near Williston, Florida. The Autopilot system failed to detect the white side of the turning truck against a brightly lit sky, resulting in the car passing under the trailer and the death of the driver.
cases.notes: NTSB determined probable cause was the truck driver's failure to yield, combined with the car driver's inattention due to overreliance on vehicle automation, and the automation's operational design permitting prolonged disengagement.

cases.title: NHTSA Investigation on Tesla FSD Traffic Violations
cases.incident_description: NHTSA opened an investigation into Tesla's Full Self-Driving (FSD) system following reports of traffic safety violations. The incidents involved vehicles proceeding through red traffic signals and driving against the proper direction of travel, including entering opposing lanes and wrong-way driving.
cases.notes: Liability allocation is estimated based on the ongoing NHTSA investigation, attributing primary responsibility to the AI provider (Tesla) for system failures and partial responsibility to the human operator for supervision failures.

cases.title: KNKT Investigation on Boeing 737 MAX MCAS Lion Air JT610
cases.incident_description: Lion Air Flight 610, a Boeing 737 MAX 8, crashed into the Java Sea shortly after takeoff due to erroneous activation of the Maneuvering Characteristics Augmentation System (MCAS). The MCAS was triggered by a single mis-calibrated Angle of Attack (AOA) sensor, repeatedly forcing the aircraft's nose down. The flight crew was unable to counteract the system due to lack of training and documentation on MCAS.
cases.notes: The KNKT final report identified multiple contributing factors, including Boeing's design of MCAS relying on a single sensor, Lion Air's maintenance issues with the AOA sensor, and the flight crew's response.

cases.title: Wong et al. Validation of Epic Sepsis Model
cases.incident_description: An external validation study by Wong et al. found that the widely implemented proprietary Epic Sepsis Model poorly predicted sepsis in hospitalized patients. The model demonstrated low sensitivity and generated a high burden of false alerts, raising concerns about its real-world performance and calibration.

telemetry_signals.citation: Wong et al., JAMA Intern Med. 2021;181(8):1065-1070

cases.notes: Liability is allocated to the AI provider (Epic) as the model's poor performance was inherent to the proprietary system, though no formal legal liability was adjudicated.

cases.title: Google Flu Trends Overestimation of 2012-2013 Flu Season
cases.incident_description: Google Flu Trends (GFT) significantly overestimated the peak of the 2012-2013 flu season in the United States, predicting more than double the actual proportion of doctor visits for influenza-like illness compared to CDC data. The failure was attributed to changes in Google's search algorithm, such as the introduction of autosuggest, and the model's vulnerability to overfitting seasonal terms unrelated to the flu, leading to a phenomenon described as 'big data hubris'.

system_architecture.affected_party: Public health officials and general public relying on the data

telemetry_signals.citation: Butler, D. When Google got flu wrong. Nature 494, 155-156 (2013)

cases.notes: As an academic/research failure rather than a legal liability case, causation and liability are assigned 100% to the AI provider (Google) who developed and deployed the flawed model.

cases.title: Roberts et al. on Spurious Correlations in COVID-19 Chest X-ray Models
cases.incident_description: A systematic review by Roberts et al. (2021) found that hundreds of machine learning models developed to detect COVID-19 from chest X-rays and CT scans were clinically useless due to methodological flaws and underlying biases. Specifically, models learned spurious correlations, such as identifying the source hospital or dataset rather than the disease, as demonstrated by Maguolo et al. where models could 'diagnose' COVID-19 even when the lung region was entirely excluded from the images.

telemetry_signals.citation: Roberts et al., Nat Mach Intell 3, 199–217 (2021)

cases.notes: This is an academic study highlighting systemic failures in AI model development due to biased datasets and spurious correlations, rather than a specific legal or regulatory incident.

cases.title: Prygodicz v Commonwealth of Australia (No 2)
cases.incident_description: The Australian government implemented an automated debt assessment and recovery system (Robodebt) that used income averaging from the Australian Taxation Office to calculate overpayments of social security benefits. The system unlawfully raised, demanded, and recovered debts from hundreds of thousands of welfare recipients, causing significant financial hardship and distress. The Federal Court approved a $1.8 billion settlement, including $112 million in compensation, after finding the government had no proper legal basis for the scheme.

telemetry_signals.citation: Prygodicz v Commonwealth of Australia (No 2) [2021] FCA 634

cases.notes: The Federal Court approved a settlement where the Commonwealth conceded it did not have a proper legal basis to raise, demand or recover asserted debts based on income averaging from ATO data.

cases.title: Cahoo v. SAS Analytics Inc.
cases.incident_description: Michigan's Unemployment Insurance Agency implemented an automated system called MiDAS to detect unemployment fraud. From October 2013 to August 2015, the system falsely accused over 34,000 people of fraud, with a 93% error rate, automatically terminating benefits and assessing severe monetary penalties without human review or adequate notice.

telemetry_signals.citation: Cahoo v. SAS Analytics Inc., 912 F.3d 887 (6th Cir. 2019)

cases.notes: The Sixth Circuit affirmed the denial of qualified immunity for state officials regarding due process claims, recognizing the automated system's role in depriving claimants of property without adequate notice.

cases.title: Dutch Data Protection Authority v. Tax and Customs Administration
cases.incident_description: The Dutch Tax and Customs Administration used an algorithmic system to create risk profiles of individuals applying for childcare benefits. The system used dual nationality as a risk factor, leading to tens of thousands of parents, mostly from ethnic minorities and low-income families, being falsely accused of fraud and forced to repay benefits. This resulted in severe financial hardship and the eventual resignation of the Dutch government.
cases.notes: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) fined the Tax Administration €2.75 million for discriminatory data processing and €3.7 million for the illegal fraud blacklist.

cases.title: Rotterdam Court of Auditors and Algorithm Audit on Municipality of Rotterdam
cases.incident_description: The Municipality of Rotterdam used a machine learning algorithm (xgboost) to profile welfare recipients and predict fraud risk. The system used subjective and proxy variables (like illiteracy, gender, and language skills) which led to discriminatory profiling, particularly against vulnerable groups like single mothers and immigrants.

telemetry_signals.citation: Algorithm Audit, Risk Profiling for Social Welfare Re-examination (2023)

cases.notes: Liability allocated to the deployer (Municipality of Rotterdam) as they operated the system and made the final decisions to investigate, though Accenture initially developed it.

cases.title: JCWI v. UK Home Office
cases.incident_description: The UK Home Office used a 'streaming tool' algorithm to sort visa applications, assigning them a red, amber, or green risk rating. The algorithm used nationality as a factor, automatically assigning higher risk ratings to certain 'suspect' nationalities, which led to more intensive scrutiny and higher refusal rates. Following a legal challenge by the Joint Council for the Welfare of Immigrants (JCWI) supported by Foxglove, the Home Office agreed to scrap the algorithm before a judicial review could take place.
cases.notes: The Home Office agreed to abandon the algorithm and redesign the process following the legal challenge, effectively accepting responsibility for the discriminatory system.

cases.title: DOJ Scrutiny of Allegheny County Department of Human Services
cases.incident_description: The Allegheny Family Screening Tool (AFST), a predictive risk model used by the Allegheny County Department of Human Services to assess child welfare cases, faced scrutiny for potential bias. Investigations revealed the algorithm disproportionately flagged Black children for mandatory investigations and potentially discriminated against families with disabilities by using data points related to mental health and public benefits. The U.S. Justice Department's Civil Rights Division began scrutinizing the tool following civil rights complaints.

system_architecture.deployer_org: Allegheny County Department of Human Services

cases.notes: Liability is allocated to the deployer (Allegheny County) as they implemented and maintained the tool, though no formal liability has been adjudicated yet.

cases.title: NTSB Investigation on Tesla Autopilot Mountain View Crash
cases.incident_description: A 2017 Tesla Model X operating on Autopilot steered into a highway gore area and struck a damaged crash attenuator in Mountain View, California. The driver, Walter Huang, was killed in the crash. The NTSB determined the probable cause was the Autopilot system steering the vehicle into the gore area due to system limitations, combined with the driver's lack of response due to distraction and overreliance on the system.
cases.notes: NTSB determined probable cause was Tesla Autopilot system limitations and driver distraction/overreliance, with contributing factors including ineffective driver monitoring and a damaged crash attenuator (external actor).

cases.title: NTSB Investigation on Tesla Autopilot Delray Beach Fatal Crash
cases.incident_description: A 2018 Tesla Model 3 operating on Autopilot collided with a crossing tractor-semitrailer in Delray Beach, Florida, resulting in the death of the Tesla driver. The Autopilot system failed to detect the crossing truck, did not initiate automatic emergency braking, and did not issue a forward collision warning. The driver, overreliant on the automation, had his hands off the steering wheel for 7.7 seconds prior to the crash and failed to take evasive action.
cases.notes: NTSB determined probable cause was the truck driver's failure to yield, combined with the car driver's inattention due to overreliance on automation. Tesla's system design and NHTSA's lack of oversight were contributing factors.

cases.title: BEA Investigation on Air France Flight 447
cases.incident_description: Air France Flight 447 crashed into the Atlantic Ocean after ice crystals blocked the pitot tubes, causing the autopilot to disconnect. The flight crew failed to appropriately handle the sudden handover of control, leading to an aerodynamic stall from which they did not recover.

telemetry_signals.citation: BEA Final Report on Air France Flight 447
telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: BEA Final Report on the accident on 1st June 2009 to the Airbus A330-203 registered F-GZCP operated by Air France flight AF 447 Rio de Janeiro – Paris (July 2012)

cases.notes: The BEA final report highlighted a combination of technical failure (pitot tube icing causing autopilot disconnect) and human error (inappropriate control inputs leading to stall) as the causes of the accident.

cases.title: NTSB Investigation of USS John S. McCain Collision
cases.incident_description: The USS John S. McCain collided with the tanker Alnic MC in the Singapore Strait, resulting in 10 deaths and 48 injuries. The collision was caused by a lack of effective operational oversight, insufficient training, and inadequate bridge operating procedures regarding the ship's Integrated Bridge and Navigation System (IBNS). An unintentional transfer of steering control and a perceived loss of steering led to the incident.
cases.notes: The NTSB report primarily blamed the U.S. Navy for lack of oversight and training, while noting the complex design of the IBNS contributed to the confusion.

cases.title: Reid v. Jefferson Parish Sheriff's Office
cases.incident_description: Randal Quran Reid, a 29-year-old Black man, was wrongfully arrested in Georgia and held in jail for nearly a week based on warrants from Louisiana. The warrants were issued after police in Jefferson Parish and Baton Rouge relied on a false facial recognition match using Clearview AI to identify him as a suspect in credit card thefts. Reid sued the authorities and eventually reached a $200,000 settlement.

telemetry_signals.citation: New York Times, 'Thousands of Dollars for Something I Didn't Do' (2023)

cases.notes: Liability is allocated 100% to the deployer (Jefferson Parish Sheriff's Office) based on the $200,000 settlement paid to Reid for the wrongful arrest.

cases.title: Parks v. McCormac
cases.incident_description: Nijeer Parks was wrongfully arrested and imprisoned for 10 days in 2019 after Woodbridge police relied exclusively on a false match from a facial recognition system. The system misidentified him as a shoplifting suspect based on a blurry photo from a fake driver's license. The case was dismissed for lack of evidence, and Parks subsequently sued the police and township, eventually reaching a $300,000 settlement.
cases.notes: Liability allocation is based on the $300,000 settlement paid by the Woodbridge township and police officials for the wrongful arrest.

cases.title: Hood v OpenAI
cases.incident_description: ChatGPT falsely claimed that Brian Hood, the Mayor of Hepburn Shire Council in Australia, had served time in prison for bribery. In reality, Hood was the whistleblower who exposed the foreign bribery scandal involving a subsidiary of the Reserve Bank of Australia. Hood's lawyers sent a concerns notice to OpenAI, threatening a defamation lawsuit, which was later settled out of court.
cases.notes: The case was settled out of court before a formal lawsuit was filed, with OpenAI agreeing to remove the incorrect content.

cases.title: Battle v. Microsoft Corporation
cases.incident_description: Air Force veteran Jeffery Battle sued Microsoft for defamation after discovering that searching his name on Bing generated a short blurb that conflated his biography with that of Jeffrey Battle, a member of the 'Portland Seven' who tried to join the Taliban. Battle sought monetary compensation and the removal of the false information from Bing search results.
cases.notes: On October 23, 2024, Judge Griggsby granted Microsoft's motion to compel arbitration and stayed the proceedings in court, meaning the case is unlikely to generate a ruling that could be applied to other defamation cases.

cases.title: Jonathan Turley v. OpenAI
cases.incident_description: ChatGPT falsely accused law professor Jonathan Turley of sexual harassment during a class trip to Alaska. The AI hallucinated a non-existent 2018 Washington Post article to substantiate the claim.
cases.notes: No formal lawsuit was filed, but the incident is well-documented in major news outlets. Liability is allocated entirely to the AI provider for the hallucination.

cases.title: ICO Opinion on London Metropolitan Police Live Facial Recognition
cases.incident_description: The London Metropolitan Police Service (MPS) deployed Live Facial Recognition (LFR) technology in public spaces between 2016 and 2019. The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated these trials and issued an Opinion in 2019, followed by another in 2021, highlighting concerns about the necessity, proportionality, and fairness of processing biometric data, as well as potential biases and lack of transparency.
cases.notes: The ICO issued opinions highlighting data protection concerns regarding the Met Police's use of LFR, while the Court of Appeal in Bridges v SWP found similar deployments unlawful due to inadequate legal framework and failure to comply with the Public Sector Equality Duty.

cases.title: EHRC investigation into DWP universal credit algorithm
cases.incident_description: The UK Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) deployed a machine learning algorithm to detect potential fraud in Universal Credit advances. The Equality and Human Rights Commission (EHRC) launched an investigation into whether the DWP failed to anticipate and make reasonable adjustments for disabled people with a mental impairment during health assessment determinations, and whether the algorithm showed bias based on age, disability, marital status, and nationality.
cases.notes: The EHRC is investigating the DWP for potential breaches of equality law regarding the algorithm's impact on disabled individuals and other protected groups.

cases.title: Sheffield City Council automated welfare risk scoring concerns
cases.incident_description: Sheffield City Council faced concerns regarding its use of automated welfare risk scoring algorithms in 2022. The system was intended to identify individuals at risk of needing welfare assistance, but raised concerns about transparency, bias, and the potential for discriminatory outcomes against vulnerable populations.

telemetry_signals.citation: News reporting on UK welfare algorithms (2022)

cases.notes: Incident details are sparse and difficult to verify from primary sources; concerns were raised about automated welfare risk scoring but formal adjudication is not evident.

cases.title: State v. Pickett COMPAS challenge
cases.incident_description: In 2018, Ira Lee Pickett challenged the use of the COMPAS risk assessment algorithm in his sentencing in Florida. The Third District Court of Appeal of Florida affirmed the lower court's decision.
cases.notes: The incident cannot be fully verified as a COMPAS challenge based on the primary source found, which is a per curiam affirmance without a published opinion.

cases.title: Andersen v. Stability AI Ltd.
cases.incident_description: Artists filed a class-action lawsuit against Stability AI, Midjourney, DeviantArt, and Runway AI, alleging that the companies used their copyrighted artworks without permission to train AI image generators like Stable Diffusion. The plaintiffs claim the AI models directly infringe on their copyrights by copying and ingesting billions of training images, allowing the models to generate outputs that mimic the protected expression of the original artists.

telemetry_signals.citation: Andersen v. Stability AI Ltd., 23-cv-00201 (N.D. Cal. 2024)

cases.notes: Liability allocation is based on the ongoing litigation where the primary claim of direct copyright infringement against the AI providers survived the motion to dismiss.

cases.title: Getty Images v. Stability AI
cases.incident_description: Getty Images sued Stability AI in the UK High Court, alleging that Stability AI scraped millions of copyrighted images and associated metadata from Getty's websites without permission to train its Stable Diffusion image generation model. The lawsuit claimed primary and secondary copyright infringement, database right infringement, trademark infringement, and passing off. The court largely dismissed the copyright claims, ruling that the AI model itself does not store or reproduce the copyrighted works and thus is not an 'infringing copy' under UK law, though it found limited trademark infringement regarding the generation of Getty's watermarks.

telemetry_signals.citation: Getty Images (US) Inc & Ors v. Stability AI Limited [2025] EWHC 2863 (Ch)

cases.notes: The UK High Court largely dismissed the copyright infringement claims, finding that the AI model does not store or reproduce the copyrighted works, but found limited trademark infringement.

cases.title: Authors Guild v. OpenAI
cases.incident_description: The Authors Guild and several prominent authors filed a class-action lawsuit against OpenAI and Microsoft, alleging that the companies used their copyrighted books without permission to train GPT models. The plaintiffs claim that ChatGPT generates detailed plot summaries that constitute derivative works and infringe on their copyrights.
cases.notes: Liability allocation is estimated based on the ongoing lawsuit against the AI provider for copyright infringement.

cases.title: Doe v. GitHub, Inc.
cases.incident_description: Plaintiffs filed a class-action lawsuit alleging that GitHub Copilot and OpenAI Codex, AI-based coding tools, were trained on and output copyrighted open-source code without adhering to the applicable license terms, such as providing attribution or copyright notices.
cases.notes: Liability allocation is estimated as the case is ongoing; both GitHub (deployer) and OpenAI (provider) are named as defendants for their respective roles in Copilot and Codex.

cases.title: NTSB Investigation on Uber ATG Pedestrian Fatality
cases.incident_description: An Uber Advanced Technologies Group autonomous test vehicle struck and killed a pedestrian crossing the street at night in Tempe, Arizona. The automated driving system failed to correctly classify the pedestrian and predict her path, while the safety driver was visually distracted by a personal device.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: NTSB Highway Accident Report HAR-19/03 (November 2019)

cases.notes: NTSB attributed probable cause to the safety driver's distraction, Uber's inadequate safety culture, and the pedestrian's actions. Uber settled civilly and avoided federal prosecution, while the safety driver pleaded guilty to endangerment.

cases.title: NTSB Investigation on Tesla Autopilot Walter Huang fatal crash
cases.incident_description: A 2017 Tesla Model X operating on Autopilot lost lane tracking and steered into a highway gore area, accelerating into a damaged crash attenuator. The driver, distracted by a mobile device, did not take evasive action, resulting in a fatal collision.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: NTSB Highway Accident Report HAR-20/01 (February 2020)

cases.notes: NTSB does not formally allocate liability, but identified the Autopilot system's limitations, the driver's distraction, and the highway department's failure to repair the crash attenuator as contributing factors.

cases.title: Ethiopian Airlines Flight 302 Boeing 737 MAX Crash
cases.incident_description: On March 10, 2019, Ethiopian Airlines Flight 302 crashed shortly after takeoff due to the erroneous activation of the Maneuvering Characteristics Augmentation System (MCAS). A single faulty angle-of-attack sensor triggered MCAS to repeatedly push the aircraft's nose down, overpowering the flight crew's ability to control the plane.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Ethiopian Aircraft Accident Investigation Bureau (EAIB) Final Report on Flight 302 (December 2022)

cases.notes: The EAIB and U.S. House reports attribute the crash primarily to Boeing's flawed MCAS design and the FAA's inadequate certification oversight. Boeing assumed full liability in civil settlements.

cases.title: TSB Canada investigation on Air Transat Flight 236 fuel-leak FADEC crew-handover incident
cases.incident_description: Air Transat Flight 236 experienced a fuel leak due to improper maintenance. The ECAM system alerted the crew to a fuel imbalance but did not explicitly warn of a fuel leak. The crew, not recognizing the leak, followed the fuel imbalance procedure, transferring fuel to the leaking engine, leading to dual engine flameout and an emergency glide landing in the Azores.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Aviation Accidents Prevention and Investigation Department (Portugal) Final Investigation Report 22 / ACCID / 2001 (18 October 2004)

cases.notes: Air Transat accepted responsibility and was fined by Transport Canada. Ground truth causation splits blame between maintenance (deployer), crew error (human_operator), and ECAM design (ai_provider).

cases.title: KNKT Investigation on Sriwijaya Air Flight SJ-182 Boeing 737-500 Autothrottle Anomaly
cases.incident_description: Sriwijaya Air Flight SJ-182 crashed into the Java Sea shortly after takeoff due to an autothrottle anomaly. The right thrust lever failed to reduce power due to mechanical friction, causing the left thrust lever to compensate by moving further back, resulting in severe thrust asymmetry. The delayed Cruise Thrust Split Monitor (CTSM) activation and pilot automation complacency led to a loss of control.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: KNKT Aircraft Accident Investigation Report KNKT.21.01.01.04 (November 2022)

cases.notes: The KNKT report identifies multiple contributing factors: mechanical friction in the autothrottle (Boeing/maintenance), delayed CTSM activation due to undervalued spoiler angle (Boeing/maintenance), inadequate maintenance by Sriwijaya Air, and pilot automation complacency/confirmation bias (human operator). Apportionment reflects shared responsibility among the manufacturer, airline maintenance, and flight crew.

cases.title: NHTSA Recall 23V-085: Tesla Full Self-Driving Beta
cases.incident_description: Tesla's Full Self-Driving Beta software was found to potentially infringe upon local traffic laws or customs while executing certain driving maneuvers, such as traveling through intersections during a stale yellow light or failing to come to a complete stop at stop signs. This increased the risk of a collision if the driver did not intervene.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: NHTSA Part 573 Safety Recall Report 23V-085 (February 15, 2023)

cases.notes: Tesla, as the vertically integrated manufacturer and software provider, bears full responsibility for the recall and the software defect.

cases.title: NHTSA Recall 23V-838: Tesla Autopilot Autosteer
cases.incident_description: Tesla recalled over 2 million vehicles equipped with Autopilot due to insufficient controls to prevent driver misuse. The Autosteer feature failed to adequately ensure drivers maintained continuous responsibility, leading to foreseeable disengagement and avoidable crashes.

system_architecture.affected_party: vehicle occupants and other road users

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: NHTSA Part 573 Safety Recall Report 23V-838 (December 12, 2023)

cases.notes: Liability allocation reflects Tesla's responsibility for the recall and system design defect. Ground truth causation is split between the AI provider (Tesla) for insufficient engagement controls and the human operator for failing to maintain attention as required by the L2 system.

cases.title: California DMV suspension of Cruise (GM) driverless permits after October 2023 San Francisco pedestrian-dragging incident
cases.incident_description: A Cruise autonomous vehicle operating in San Francisco struck a pedestrian who had been thrown into its path by a human hit-and-run driver. The AV's collision detection system incorrectly identified the contact as a side impact collision and executed a pullover maneuver, dragging the pedestrian 20 feet.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: California Department of Motor Vehicles, Order of Suspension, Autonomous Vehicles Deployment Permit (Oct. 24, 2023)

cases.notes: The DMV suspended Cruise's deployment permit based on the AV's post-collision response (pullover maneuver) and Cruise's failure to disclose the dragging. The primary source is the DMV suspension order.

cases.title: NTSB Investigation on Tesla Autopilot Williston Florida Fatal Crash
cases.incident_description: A Tesla Model S operating on Autopilot collided with a turning tractor-trailer that crossed its path. The Autopilot system failed to detect the white side of the trailer against a brightly lit sky, and the driver did not intervene, resulting in a fatal underride crash.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: NTSB Highway Accident Report HAR-17/02 (September 2017)

cases.notes: The NTSB determined the probable cause was the truck driver's failure to yield, combined with the car driver's inattention due to overreliance on vehicle automation. The NTSB does not assign legal liability, so liability allocation is based on the truck driver's failure to yield.

cases.title: Waymo NHTSA Recall 24E-013 for Software Collision Avoidance Issue
cases.incident_description: Two Waymo autonomous vehicles operating in Phoenix, Arizona, made contact with a pickup truck being improperly towed backwards and at an angle. The Waymo Automated Driving System (ADS) incorrectly predicted the future motion of the towed vehicle, leading to low-speed collisions.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: NHTSA Part 573 Safety Recall Report 24E-013 (February 13, 2024)

cases.notes: Waymo LLC is both the model provider and deployer. The failure was entirely due to the ADS software incorrectly predicting the towed vehicle's motion, placing 100% causation and liability on the AI provider.

cases.title: Mata v. Avianca Inc.
cases.incident_description: Plaintiff's attorneys used ChatGPT to conduct legal research for an affirmation in opposition to a motion to dismiss. The AI system hallucinated non-existent judicial opinions with fake quotes and citations, which the attorneys then submitted to the court without verification.

system_architecture.human_operator: Steven A. Schwartz and Peter LoDuca
system_architecture.affected_party: Roberto Mata and the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Mata v. Avianca, Inc., 678 F. Supp. 3d 443 (S.D.N.Y. 2023)

cases.notes: The court sanctioned the attorneys and their law firm for acting in subjective bad faith by submitting fake cases generated by ChatGPT and standing by them after their existence was questioned. The AI provider was not held liable.

cases.title: Park v. Kim, 91 F.4th 610 (2d Cir. 2024)
cases.incident_description: An attorney representing the plaintiff in an appeal used ChatGPT to find case law to support her argument. The AI system hallucinated a non-existent state court decision, which the attorney then cited in her reply brief without verifying its existence. The Second Circuit Court of Appeals discovered the fabrication and referred the attorney to the court's Grievance Panel for potential disciplinary action.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Park v. Kim, 91 F.4th 610 (2d Cir. 2024)

cases.notes: The court explicitly held the human operator (the attorney) solely responsible under Rule 11 for failing to verify the AI-generated citation.

cases.title: Wadsworth v. Walmart Inc. (D. Wyo. Feb 2025)
cases.incident_description: Attorneys at Morgan & Morgan used an in-house AI platform, MX2.law, to draft motions in limine in a products liability case. The AI generated eight non-existent case citations, which the attorneys submitted to the court without verifying their accuracy.

telemetry_signals.citation: Wadsworth v. Walmart Inc., No. 2:23-cv-118-KHR (D. Wyo. Feb. 24, 2025)
telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Wadsworth v. Walmart Inc., No. 2:23-cv-118-KHR (D. Wyo. Feb. 24, 2025)

cases.notes: The court sanctioned the individual attorneys for failing to verify the AI-generated citations, explicitly declining to sanction the law firm because it had policies against such use.

cases.title: People v. Zachariah C. Crabill
cases.incident_description: Colorado attorney Zachariah Crabill used ChatGPT to draft a motion to set aside judgment and included fabricated case citations generated by the AI. He filed the motion without verifying the citations and later falsely attributed the errors to a legal intern before admitting to using ChatGPT.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: People v. Zachariah C. Crabill, 23PDJ067 (Colo. O.P.D.J. Nov. 22, 2023)

cases.notes: The attorney was solely held liable for failing to verify the citations and for professional misconduct.

cases.title: DPP v. AB (Australia) - Victorian solicitor referred to legal-services board after AI-generated case citations submitted in family-court matter
cases.incident_description: A Victorian solicitor appearing as an agent in a family court matter tendered a list and summary of authorities generated using an AI tool incorporated in his legal practice management software. The solicitor did not verify the accuracy of the information, and the authorities identified in the list and summary did not exist. The solicitor was referred to the Victorian Legal Services Board and Commissioner.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Dayal [2024] FedCFamC2F 1166 (27 August 2024)

cases.notes: The court found the solicitor responsible for failing to verify the AI-generated citations and referred him to the Victorian Legal Services Board, which subsequently varied his practising certificate.

cases.title: Massachusetts AG Settlement with Joshua Hyman / Sumiyo Inc. over AI Lawyer Marketing
cases.incident_description: Joshua Hyman and Sumiyo Inc. used an AI system to generate lawyer marketing letters that falsely claimed an attorney-client relationship. The Massachusetts Attorney General reached a settlement regarding these deceptive practices.
