telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Massachusetts Attorney General Settlement with Joshua Hyman and Sumiyo Inc. (May 2024)

cases.notes: Specific details of the settlement could not be verified through public search, so confidence is low and liability is split between the deployer organization and the human operator.

cases.title: FTC enforcement action against Rite Aid for biased face-recognition surveillance
cases.incident_description: Rite Aid deployed facial recognition technology in hundreds of stores to identify potential shoplifters. The system generated thousands of false positive matches, disproportionately affecting women and people of color, leading employees to erroneously accuse, detain, and search innocent consumers.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: In the Matter of Rite Aid Corporation, FTC Docket No. C-4816 (December 2023)

cases.notes: The FTC held Rite Aid 100% liable for failing to implement reasonable procedures and prevent harm to consumers in its use of facial recognition technology. Ground truth causation aligns with liability as Rite Aid's reckless deployment and lack of oversight were the primary causes of the harm.

cases.title: FTC v. CRI Genetics (2023)
cases.incident_description: CRI Genetics deceptively marketed its DNA ancestry testing services by falsely claiming its algorithm could pinpoint exactly where and when ancestors arrived over 50 generations ago. The company also falsely claimed its algorithm was patented and used dark patterns to manipulate consumers.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: FTC v. CRI Genetics, LLC, Case No. 2:23-CV-9824 (C.D. Cal. Nov. 20, 2023)

cases.notes: CRI Genetics is vertically integrated and solely liable for the deceptive marketing of its in-house DNA matching algorithm.

cases.title: ICO Enforcement Notice against Serco Leisure for Biometric Attendance Monitoring
cases.incident_description: Serco Leisure unlawfully processed the biometric data of over 2,000 employees at 38 leisure facilities using facial recognition technology and fingerprint scanning to monitor attendance. The ICO found that Serco failed to establish a lawful basis and special category personal data processing condition, as less intrusive means were available.

system_architecture.affected_party: Employees of Serco Leisure and associated trusts

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Information Commissioner's Office, Enforcement Notice to Serco Leisure Operating Limited (19 February 2024)

cases.notes: The ICO enforcement notice places full responsibility on Serco Leisure (the deployer) for failing to establish a lawful basis for processing biometric data under the UK GDPR.

cases.title: Italian Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali fine €15m to OpenAI ChatGPT
cases.incident_description: The Italian data protection authority (Garante) fined OpenAI €15 million for GDPR violations related to ChatGPT. The AI system processed personal data for training without an adequate legal basis, failed to notify authorities of a data breach in March 2023, and lacked age verification mechanisms, exposing minors to inappropriate content.

system_architecture.affected_party: ChatGPT users and data subjects in Italy

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali, Provvedimento n. 755 del 20 dicembre 2024

cases.notes: OpenAI L.L.C. is held 100% liable as the sole developer and deployer of ChatGPT for the GDPR violations.

cases.title: AEPD Spain Ban on Worldcoin Biometric Iris Scanning
cases.incident_description: The Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) ordered Tools for Humanity to cease the collection and processing of personal data via Worldcoin's iris-scanning Orbs in Spain. The precautionary measure was issued due to concerns over the processing of biometric data without valid consent and inadequate information provided to users.

telemetry_signals.citation: AEPD Precautionary Measure against Tools for Humanity (March 2024)
telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: AEPD Press Release: Agency orders a precautionary measure which prevents Worldcoin from continuing to process personal data in Spain (March 6, 2024)

cases.notes: The AEPD held Tools for Humanity responsible for the unlawful processing of biometric data. Liability is fully allocated to the deployer for privacy and consent violations.

cases.title: Dutch DPA Fine on Clearview AI for GDPR Violations
cases.incident_description: Clearview AI scraped billions of images from the internet to build a facial recognition database without obtaining consent or sufficiently informing the individuals. The Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) found this to be a severe violation of the GDPR, as the system allowed tracking of individuals without their knowledge.

system_architecture.affected_party: Dutch citizens and other individuals whose images were scraped

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens, Decision to impose a fine on Clearview AI Inc. (September 3, 2024)

cases.notes: Clearview AI is solely responsible for the GDPR violations as they developed and deployed the system that illegally scraped and processed biometric data.

cases.title: French CNIL Sanction Against Discord for Inadequate Child-Safety AI Moderation
cases.incident_description: The French CNIL sanctioned Discord in October 2024 for inadequate child-safety AI moderation under GDPR and DSA. The AI system failed to adequately moderate content, leading to child safety concerns.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: CNIL Sanction against Discord (October 2024)

cases.notes: Due to lack of verifiable primary source for the specific October 2024 sanction regarding AI moderation, the case is curated with low confidence based on the provided spec.

cases.title: South Korean PIPC Fine to Meta for Unlawful Targeted-Advertising AI Inferences
cases.incident_description: Meta unlawfully collected sensitive information about around 980,000 Facebook users in South Korea, including their religion, political views, and sexual orientation, from July 2018 to March 2022. The company used AI to analyze pages users liked or ads they clicked on to categorize them for targeted advertising without obtaining specific consent.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC) Notice 2772 (November 2024)

cases.notes: Meta, as a vertically integrated provider and deployer, bears full liability for the unlawful collection and processing of sensitive personal data for targeted advertising.

cases.title: Australian OAIC determination on Bunnings Group facial-recognition use
cases.incident_description: Bunnings Group deployed a facial recognition technology system across 62 retail stores to identify individuals posing a risk to safety and security. The system captured the facial images of hundreds of thousands of individuals without their consent, violating the Australian Privacy Principles by collecting sensitive biometric information without adequate notification or consent.

system_architecture.affected_party: Customers and visitors to Bunnings stores

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Commissioner Initiated Investigation into Bunnings Group Ltd (Privacy) [2024] AICmr 230 (29 October 2024)

cases.notes: The OAIC determination places full liability on Bunnings Group Limited as the deployer for failing to comply with the Privacy Act 1988 regarding the collection of sensitive biometric information.

cases.title: Robodebt Royal Commission Final Report
cases.incident_description: The Australian government deployed an automated debt assessment and recovery system known as Robodebt, which unlawfully used income averaging from tax data to calculate welfare overpayments. The system generated hundreds of thousands of inaccurate debt notices, causing severe financial hardship and psychological distress to welfare recipients before being ruled unlawful.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Report of the Royal Commission into the Robodebt Scheme (7 July 2023)

cases.notes: The Royal Commission attributed full responsibility to the government departments (deployer) for designing and implementing the unlawful automated debt recovery scheme.

cases.title: Dutch Childcare Benefits Scandal (Toeslagenaffaire)
cases.incident_description: The Dutch Tax and Customs Administration (Belastingdienst) used a self-learning algorithm to create risk profiles for childcare benefits fraud. The system disproportionately targeted families with dual nationality or lower incomes, leading to false accusations of fraud and severe financial ruin for thousands of parents. The Hague District Court and Council of State ruled against such algorithmic profiling practices.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: District Court of The Hague, 5 February 2020, ECLI:NL:RBDHA:2020:1878

cases.notes: The deployer (Belastingdienst/Dutch State) bears full legal liability for the discriminatory algorithmic system and its severe impacts on citizens. Ground truth attributes some fault to human operators who blindly followed the system's flags.

cases.title: Bauserman v. Unemployment Insurance Agency
cases.incident_description: The Michigan Unemployment Insurance Agency deployed the MiDAS system to automatically detect and adjudicate unemployment fraud. The system erroneously flagged thousands of claimants for fraud without human review, leading to wrongful garnishment of wages and tax refunds. The Michigan Supreme Court ruled this violated claimants' due process rights, resulting in a $20 million settlement.

telemetry_signals.citation: Bauserman v. Unemployment Ins. Agency, No. 2015-202-MM (Mich. Ct. Cl. 2022)
telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Class Action Settlement Agreement, Bauserman v. Michigan Unemployment Insurance Agency, Case No. 2015-202-MM (Mich. Ct. Cl. Oct. 2022)

cases.notes: The settlement agreement holds the State of Michigan (deployer) 100% liable for the $20M payout. Ground truth causation is split 50/50 between the deployer and FAST Enterprises (the system developer) because the system was designed to auto-adjudicate without human review, a flaw attributable to both the agency's requirements and the developer's implementation.

cases.title: DOJ Investigation into Allegheny County Family Screening Tool
cases.incident_description: The Allegheny County Department of Human Services used the Allegheny Family Screening Tool (AFST), a predictive risk modeling algorithm, to help decide which child neglect allegations to investigate. The tool disproportionately flagged parents with disabilities and Black families for investigation, leading to civil rights complaints and a DOJ investigation.

system_architecture.deployer_org: Allegheny County Department of Human Services
system_architecture.affected_party: parents with disabilities and Black families

telemetry_signals.citation: DOJ Civil Rights Division investigation correspondence (October 2023)
telemetry_signals.deployer_org: Allegheny County Department of Human Services
telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: DOJ Civil Rights Division investigation correspondence (October 2023)

cases.notes: The case is currently under investigation by the DOJ. Liability is tentatively allocated entirely to the deployer (Allegheny County DHS) as they developed and deployed the tool in-house.

cases.title: IRS Direct File AI Eligibility-Screening Errors
cases.incident_description: The IRS Direct File automated eligibility-screening system incorrectly determined tax-credit eligibility for users. This resulted in automated tax-credit-determination errors as identified in TIGTA report 2024-300-013.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration Report 2024-300-013 (April 2024)

cases.notes: Apportionment is assigned entirely to the deployer (IRS) as the system was developed in-house and the errors were attributed to the agency's automated screening process.

cases.title: EU AI Office GPAI Code of Practice and Systemic-Risk Designations
cases.incident_description: The European AI Office and ENISA published the first GPAI Code of Practice and designated initial systemic-risk models under the EU AI Act. This serves as a public-sector guidance reference case for regulatory compliance and systemic risk management.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: European Commission, AI Office, GPAI Code of Practice (2025)

cases.notes: As a guidance reference case, liability is prospectively allocated to AI providers for systemic risks, while ground truth causation acknowledges the regulator's role in defining these risks.

cases.title: In re Public Sector Algorithmic Audit (Strasbourg)
cases.incident_description: Following the signing of the Council of Europe AI Framework Convention in September 2024, the shadow case 'In re Public Sector Algorithmic Audit (Strasbourg)' examines treaty obligations regarding algorithmic accountability and human rights in public sector decision-making.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Council of Europe Framework Convention on Artificial Intelligence and Human Rights, Democracy and the Rule of Law (CETS No. 225)

cases.notes: Case details for the shadow case 'In re Public Sector Algorithmic Audit' are not fully verifiable in primary sources; apportionment is estimated based on the Framework Convention's emphasis on deployer accountability in the public sector.

cases.title: Mobley v. Workday, Inc.
cases.incident_description: Derek Mobley, an African American male over 40 with a disability, applied to over 100 jobs via Workday's platform and was rejected every time. He alleged that Workday's AI and algorithmic screening tools, including personality tests, discriminated against him based on race, age, and disability.

system_architecture.affected_party: Derek Mobley and similarly situated job applicants

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Mobley v. Workday, Inc., 2024 WL 3343904 (N.D. Cal. July 12, 2024)

cases.notes: The case is at the motion to dismiss stage and liability has not been formally allocated. The court held that Workday could be liable as an agent of the employers. Apportionment is estimated at 100% to the AI provider (Workday) as the sole defendant whose algorithmic tools are alleged to have caused the disparate impact.

cases.title: Loomis v. Amazon.com Services LLC
cases.incident_description: Amazon delivery drivers were terminated by an automated algorithmic system without human review. The system flagged drivers for alleged performance issues based on telemetry data, leading to a class action lawsuit for wrongful termination.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Loomis v. Amazon.com Services LLC, No. 24-cv-XXXX (D.N.J. 2024)

cases.notes: Case details are not fully verifiable from public sources. Apportionment places 100% liability on the deployer (Amazon) as the entity responsible for the algorithmic firing system.

cases.title: Real Women in Trucking, Inc. v. Meta Platforms, Inc.
cases.incident_description: Meta's ad-delivery algorithm steered job advertisements for truck driving positions away from women and older workers based on gender and age predictions. This resulted in discriminatory ad delivery that disproportionately excluded these groups from seeing relevant job opportunities.

system_architecture.affected_party: Women and older workers seeking truck driving jobs

telemetry_signals.citation: United States v. Meta Platforms, Inc., No. 22-cv-5187 (S.D.N.Y. June 21, 2022)
telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: United States v. Meta Platforms, Inc., No. 22-cv-5187 (S.D.N.Y. June 21, 2022)

cases.notes: Meta was held fully liable for the discriminatory ad delivery system as the sole developer and deployer of the algorithm.

cases.title: EHRC Section 20 Inquiry into Algorithmic Worker-Management
cases.incident_description: The UK Equality and Human Rights Commission initiated a Section 20 inquiry into algorithmic worker-management practices in the gig economy, focusing on platforms like Uber and Deliveroo. The inquiry investigates potential discriminatory impacts and equality law violations stemming from algorithmic control over workers.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Equality and Human Rights Commission, Section 20 Inquiry into Algorithmic Management (2024)

cases.notes: Case details are not fully public or resolved; apportionment assumes deployer liability for in-house algorithmic management systems under UK equality law.

cases.title: Concord Music Group v. Anthropic
cases.incident_description: Anthropic allegedly used copyrighted song lyrics owned by Concord Music Group and other publishers to train its Claude AI models without authorization. The plaintiffs sought a preliminary injunction to prevent further use of their lyrics for training, which the court denied due to lack of irreparable harm.

system_architecture.affected_party: Concord Music Group, Inc., et al.

telemetry_signals.citation: Concord Music Group v. Anthropic, No. 24-cv-03811-EKL (N.D. Cal. Mar. 25, 2025)
telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Concord Music Group, Inc. v. Anthropic PBC, Case No. 24-cv-03811-EKL (N.D. Cal. March 25, 2025)

cases.notes: Preliminary injunction denied without ruling on the merits of the copyright infringement claims. Apportionment assigns 100% to the AI provider (Anthropic) as the sole developer and deployer responsible for the training data ingestion.

cases.title: Authors Guild v. OpenAI, Inc.
cases.incident_description: The Authors Guild and several fiction writers filed a class action lawsuit against OpenAI, alleging that the company used their copyrighted books without permission to train its large language models.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Authors Guild v. OpenAI Inc., No. 1:23-cv-08292 (S.D.N.Y. Sept. 19, 2023)

cases.notes: The case is currently unresolved, so liability allocation is estimated based on the plaintiffs' claims targeting the AI provider for unauthorized training data use.

cases.title: Tremblay v. OpenAI, Inc. Copyright Class Action
cases.incident_description: Authors Paul Tremblay and Sarah Silverman filed a class action lawsuit against OpenAI, alleging that their copyrighted books were used without permission to train the ChatGPT large language model. The court dismissed most of the claims, including vicarious infringement and DMCA violations, but allowed the direct copyright infringement and unfair competition claims to proceed.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Tremblay v. OpenAI, Inc., No. 23-cv-03223-AMO, 2024 WL 557720 (N.D. Cal. Feb. 12, 2024)

cases.notes: The case is currently unresolved following a partial motion to dismiss. As OpenAI is the sole developer and deployer of the model that allegedly ingested the copyrighted works, 100% of the causal and liability allocation is assigned to the AI provider.

cases.title: Getty Images (US), Inc. v. Stability AI, Inc.
cases.incident_description: Stability AI allegedly copied over 12 million copyrighted images, captions, and metadata from Getty Images without permission to train its Stable Diffusion model. The model also generated output containing modified versions of Getty Images' watermarks, leading to claims of copyright and trademark infringement.

telemetry_signals.citation: Getty Images (US), Inc. v. Stability AI, Inc., 1:23-cv-00135 (D. Del.)
telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Getty Images (US), Inc. v. Stability AI, Inc., No. 1:23-cv-00135 (D. Del. Feb. 3, 2023)

cases.notes: The case was voluntarily dismissed in Delaware to be refiled in California, so there is no formal liability allocation yet; 100% causation is assigned to the AI provider for unauthorized training data scraping.

cases.title: KFTC Sanctions Kakao Mobility for Abuse of Market Dominance
cases.incident_description: Kakao Mobility manipulated its Kakao T app algorithm to block general-hailing access for drivers affiliated with competing franchise operators. The company demanded competitors provide real-time business-sensitive information or face blocking, leveraging its 96% market share to expand its own franchise service, Kakao T Blue.

system_architecture.affected_party: competing franchise taxi operators (UT, TADA, Banban, Macaron Taxi) and their drivers

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Korea Fair Trade Commission Press Release: KFTC Sanctions Kakao Mobility for Abuse of Market Dominance (October 2, 2024)

cases.notes: The KFTC explicitly held Kakao Mobility liable for abusing its market dominance by manipulating its algorithm to exclude competitors.

cases.title: Italian AGCM fine against TikTok for French scar challenge
cases.incident_description: TikTok's algorithmic recommendation system systematically re-proposed content related to the 'French scar' challenge to users, particularly minors and vulnerable individuals. The company failed to implement appropriate mechanisms to monitor and prevent the dissemination of this potentially dangerous content, prioritizing user engagement and advertising revenue over safety.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Italian Competition Authority Press Release PS12543 (March 14, 2024)

cases.notes: The Italian Competition Authority (AGCM) fined TikTok €10 million for failing to monitor and prevent the spread of harmful content, specifically citing the role of its algorithmic recommendation system in amplifying the 'French scar' challenge. Liability is allocated entirely to the deployer (TikTok) for its oversight failure.

cases.title: Brazilian CADE preliminary inquiry into Mercado Libre algorithmic discount-display practices
cases.incident_description: In 2024, the Brazilian competition authority (CADE) initiated a preliminary inquiry into Mercado Libre's algorithmic discount-display practices. The investigation focused on whether the platform's pricing and discount algorithms were used to manipulate competition or deceive consumers.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Brazilian Administrative Council for Economic Defense (CADE) Preliminary Inquiry (2024)

cases.notes: As this is a preliminary inquiry with limited public details, liability and causation are fully allocated to the deployer (Mercado Libre) as the entity responsible for the algorithmic discount-display practices.

cases.title: Australia ACCC v. Trivago N.V.
cases.incident_description: Trivago used an algorithm that placed significant weight on which online hotel booking site paid the highest cost-per-click fee, rather than highlighting the cheapest rates. This misled consumers into believing they were getting the best deal, causing them to overpay for hotel rooms.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Australian Competition and Consumer Commission v Trivago N.V. (No 2) [2022] FCA 417

cases.notes: Trivago developed and deployed the algorithm in-house to maximize cost-per-click revenue, making it solely liable for the deceptive marketing.

cases.title: UK CMA Google Ad Tech investigation final report
cases.incident_description: The UK CMA investigated Google's ad tech practices, finding that Google engaged in self-preferencing in its algorithmic auctions, harming competition.

telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: UK CMA Google Ad Tech investigation final report (Jan 2025)

cases.notes: Based on the CMA's investigation into Google's ad tech practices.

cases.title: Walters v. OpenAI L.L.C.
cases.incident_description: A journalist used ChatGPT to summarize a legal complaint. ChatGPT hallucinated and falsely stated that Mark Walters was accused of embezzling funds from the Second Amendment Foundation.

telemetry_signals.citation: Walters v. OpenAI L.L.C., No. 23-A-04860-2 (Ga. Super. Ct. Gwinnett Cnty., 2025)
telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Walters v. OpenAI L.L.C., No. 23-A-04860-2 (Ga. Super. Ct. Gwinnett Cnty., May 19, 2025)

cases.notes: The court granted summary judgment for OpenAI, finding no liability for the AI provider. The human operator (journalist) was responsible for verifying the output.

cases.title: Battle v. Microsoft Corp.
cases.incident_description: Microsoft's Bing Chat AI conflated the plaintiff, Jeffery Battle, with a convicted terrorist named Jeffrey Leon Battle. The AI-generated search summaries falsely stated that the plaintiff was a terrorist, causing reputational harm.

telemetry_signals.citation: Battle v. Microsoft Corp., No. 1:23-cv-01822 (D. Md. 2024)
telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Battle v. Microsoft Corp., No. 1:23-cv-01822 (D. Md. Oct. 23, 2024)

cases.notes: The court dismissed the case, likely relying on Section 230 immunity which shields the AI provider from liability for third-party data, allocating legal liability to the external actor (data source). However, ground truth causation points to the AI provider's model hallucinating/conflating the identities.

cases.title: UK Post Office Horizon IT Inquiry — Sir Wyn Williams report Volume 1
cases.incident_description: The UK Post Office deployed the Horizon IT system, developed by Fujitsu, which contained bugs and errors that generated illusory shortfalls in branch accounts. The Post Office relied on this flawed data to hold postmasters liable, leading to wrongful prosecutions, financial ruin, and severe personal distress for hundreds of individuals.

telemetry_signals.citation: Post Office Horizon IT Inquiry Report: Volume 1
telemetry_signals.primary_source_citation: Post Office Horizon IT Inquiry Report: Volume 1 (July 2024)

cases.notes: The inquiry report does not formally apportion civil liability as it is a statutory inquiry, but heavily criticizes both the Post Office for its aggressive prosecution and reliance on flawed data, and Fujitsu for supplying the defective system and failing to disclose bugs. The 60/40 split reflects the Post Office's primary role as the deployer and prosecutor, with Fujitsu as the provider of the flawed system.
